elements.sent-2411-001: The main result of this paper is that there is sometimes a triangulated equivalence between $\sfD_Q( A )$, the $Q$-shaped derived category of an algebra $A$, and $\sfD( B )$, the classic derived category of a different algebra $B$.
elements.sent-2411-002: By construction, $\sfD_Q( A )$ consists of $Q$-shaped diagrams of $A$-modules for a suitable small category $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our result concerns the case where $Q$ consists of shifts of indecomposable projective modules over a self-injective $\ZZ$-graded algebra $\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-004: A notable special case is the result by Iyama, Kato, and Miyachi that $\sfD_N( A )$, the $N$-derived category of $A$, is triangulated equivalent to $\sfD\big( T_{ N-1 ( A ) \big)$, the classic derived category of $T_{ N-1 ( A )$, which denotes upper diagonal $( N-1 ) \times ( N-1 )$-matrices over $A$.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Several other special cases will also be discussed.
elements.sent-2411-006: The main result of this paper, Theorem \ref{thm:A below, is that there is sometimes a triangulated equivalence \begin{equation}\sfD_Q( A ) \cong \sfD( B ).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-007: On the left hand side, $\sfD_Q( A )$ is the $Q$-shaped derived category of an algebra $A$ which by construction consists of $Q$-shaped diagrams of $A$-modules for a suitable small category $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-008: On the right hand side, $\sfD( B )$ is the classic derived category of a different algebra $B$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Theorem \ref{thm:A concerns the case where $Q$ consists of shifts of indecomposable projective modules over a self-injective $\ZZ$-graded algebra $\Lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-010: A notable special case is the following triangulated equivalence, originally due to \cite[prop.\ 4.11]{IKM with a precursor appearing in \cite[thm.\ 3.1]{Kajiura-Saito-Takahashi . \begin{equation}\label{equ:N-derived} \sfD_N( A ) \cong \sfD\big( T_{ N-1 }( A ) \big)\end{equation} On the left hand side, $\sfD_N( A )$ is the derived category of $N$-complexes of $A$-modules in which the composition of any $N$ consecutive differentials is zero; see \cite[def.\ 3.6]{IKM .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: On the right hand side, $T_{ N-1 ( A )$ is upper diagonal $( N-1 ) \times ( N-1 )$-matrices over $A$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The category $\sfD_Q( A )$ was defined and investigated in \cite{HJ-jlms and \cite{HJ-tams building on ideas by Iyama and Minamoto \cite[sec.\ 2]{IM ; see \cite{HJ-intro for a brief introduction.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: If $Q$ is given by Figure \ref{fig:linear_category with the relations that any two consecutive arrows compose to zero, then $\sfD_Q( A )$ is {\em equal to the classic derived category $\sfD( A )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: On the other hand, if $Q$ is given by a single vertex and a loop squaring to zero, then $\sfD_Q( A )$ is the derived category of differential $A$-modules, which is very far from being equivalent to a classic derived category.
elements.sent-2411-015: This paper concerns an intermediate situation which we now describe.
elements.sent-2411-016: The following setup is due to Yamaura \cite[sec.\ 3.1]{Yamaura .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Under this setup, $Q$ is a category for which the $Q$-shaped derived category makes sense, see Remark \ref{rem:Q_works , and we prove the following.}
elements.sent-2411-019: This builds on \cite[prop.\ 3.3]{Yamaura , which shows that $T$ is a tilting object of $\sGr \Lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-020: To obtain the equivalence \eqref{equ:N-derived as a special case of Theorem \ref{thm:A , set $\Lambda = k[ X ]/( X^N )$ with $X$ in degree $1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Then $Q$ is the category given by Figure \ref{fig:linear_category with the relations that any $N$ consecutive arrows compose to zero, so $\sfD_Q( A )$ is $\sfD_N( A )$, the derived category of $N$-complexes.}

elements.sent-2411-026: Let $R$ be a graded ring, by which we mean a $\ZZ$-graded ring.
elements.sent-2411-027: We denote by $\Modu R$ and $\Gr R$ the categories of right modules and graded right modules respectively.
elements.sent-2411-028: We use $\modu R$ and $\gr R$ to denote their respective subcategories of finitely presented objects and finitely presented projective objects in these categories are denoted by $\proj R$ and $\proj^\ZZ R$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Given a graded $R$-module $M$ we can form the shifted module $M(i)$ with $M(i)_j = M_{i+j}$.

elements.sent-2411-031: In the special case that $R$ is non-negatively graded, i.e.\ $R = \bigoplus_{i\geq 0 R_i$ then given $M\in \Gr R$ the subgroup $M_{\geq n = \bigoplus_{i\geq n M_i$ is a graded submodule for any $n\in \ZZ$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-033: Let us now fix the setting we will work in.
elements.sent-2411-034: Throughout we work over an algebraically closed field $k$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The assumption that $k$ is algebraically closed is only required to apply \cite{Yamaura *{Theorem~3.3 , i.e.\ the other results are valid over any field $k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: We let $\Lambda$ be a finite dimensional graded $k$-algebra, $\{e_0,\ldots,e_n\}$ a complete set of primitive orthogonal idempotents for $\Lambda_0$, and we set \begin{equation}\ell = \sup \Lambda = \max \{i \mid \Lambda_i\neq 0\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-037: We assume that $\Lambda$ is concentrated in non-negative degrees.
elements.sent-2411-038: We write the simple module corresponding to $e_i$ as $S_i$ and it has projective cover $P_i$.
elements.sent-2411-039: The simples and projectives are naturally graded modules and we view them as such.
elements.sent-2411-040: We note that the $S_i$ are concentrated in degree $0$ and, restricting the action of $\Lambda$ to $\Lambda_0$, are precisely the simple $\Lambda_0$-modules.
elements.sent-2411-041: Moreover, $P_i$ is the base change of the corresponding projective $\Lambda_0$-module to $\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Associated to $\Lambda$ is the companion category $\sfC$ with set of objects $\ZZ$ and morphisms $\sfC(i,j) = \Lambda_{j-i}$ with composition induced by the multiplication on $\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-043: The idempotent completion of $\sfC$ is the full subcategory \begin{equation}Q = \{P_i(j) \; \mid \; 1\leq i \leq n  and  j\in \ZZ\}\end{equation} of $\Gr \Lambda$ and both of these categories are Morita equivalent to $\proj^\ZZ \Lambda$ i.e.\ \begin{equation}\Gr \Lambda = \Modu \sfC = \Modu Q.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: Let us denote by $\mfr$ the radical of $Q$ (in the sense of \cite{Street ), which is also the radical of $\Lambda$ interpreted through the above lens.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We denote the $k$-dual of a vector space $V$ by $V^\ast$, in particular we denote the dual of $\Lambda$ by $\Lambda^\ast$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Viewing $Q$ as the idempotent completion of $\sfC$ the first four statements are evident.

elements.sent-2411-054: We continue with the setup as above, so $\Lambda$ is a non-negatively graded $k$-algebra and we associate to it categories $\sfC$ and $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Then we can consider the graded ring $\Lambda \otimes_k A$, where the grading is induced by the one from $\Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Given a small $k$-linear category $\sfD$ we let $\Modu \sfD$ denote the category of right $\sfD$-modules \begin{equation}\Modu \sfD = [\sfD^\op, \Modu k]_k\end{equation} i.e.\ the category of $k$-linear functors from $\sfD^\op$ to $\Modu k$.
elements.sent-2411-058: We can form the base change of $\sfD$ to $A$ denoted $\sfD\otimes_k A$ which is the category with the same objects as $\sfD$ and for $d,d'\in \sfD$ \begin{equation}(\sfD\otimes_k A)(d,d') = \sfD(d,d')\otimes_k A\end{equation} with the obvious composition induced by the composition of $\sfD$ and multiplication of $A$.
elements.sent-2411-059: As usual, we will drop the $k$ from the subscript when it is clear (and we feel like it).
elements.sent-2411-060: We have an obvious equivalence \begin{equation}\Modu (\sfD\otimes A) \cong [\sfD^\op, \Modu A]_k\end{equation} between modules over the base change of $\sfD$ to $A$ and $k$-linear functors from $\sfD^\op$ to $\Modu A$ (this is just the hom-tensor adjunction).
elements.sent-2411-061: There are equivalences of categories \begin{equation}\Modu (Q\otimes A) \cong \Gr(\Lambda \otimes A) \cong \Modu (\sfC\otimes A).\end{equation}. \end{lem.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The category $\Gr(\Lambda \otimes A)$ has $\proj^\ZZ (\Lambda\otimes A)$ as a set of finitely presented projective generators and so \begin{equation}\Gr(\Lambda \otimes A) \cong \Modu(\proj^\ZZ (\Lambda\otimes A)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-063: Now $\proj^\ZZ (\Lambda\otimes A)$ is Morita equivalent to the full subcategory consisting of the rank $1$ free modules $\Lambda\otimes A(i)$ for $i\in \ZZ$ (the former being the additive closure of the latter).
elements.sent-2411-064: We have \begin{equation}\Hom_{\Gr(\Lambda \otimes A)}(\Lambda\otimes A(i), \Lambda\otimes A(j)) \cong (\Lambda\otimes A)_{j-i} = \Lambda_{j-i}\otimes A = (\sfC\otimes A)(i,j)\end{equation} i.e.\ the category of rank $1$ free modules is nothing but $\sfC \otimes A$, which gives the second equivalence in the statement.

elements.sent-2411-065: Since $\sfC$ and $Q$ are Morita equivalent so are $Q\otimes A$ and $\sfC\otimes A$ and so we are done.

elements.sent-2411-067: As usual $i^*$ has adjoints $i^* \dashv i_* \dashv i^!$ which automatically, by the above, are similarly compatible with the equivalence to graded modules.

elements.sent-2411-136: One could, or perhaps even should, compare this to the case of $N$-complexes.
elements.sent-2411-137: Recall that $N$-complexes over $A$ are another name for objects of $\Gr A[x]/x^n$ where $x$ has degree $1$.
elements.sent-2411-138: From, for instance, a $4$-complex we can obtain three different chain complexes by writing $x^4$ as $x\cdot x^3$, $x^2\cdot x^2$, and $x^3\cdot x$ and this gives three different notions of cohomology for such a gadget.
elements.sent-2411-139: It turns out these describe the derived category of $4$-complexes: it is equivalent to $\sfD(kA_3\otimes A)$.
elements.sent-2411-140: Through this lens, $Q_3$ is a different way of encoding the same interactions between three chain complexes.
elements.sent-2411-141: Let $\Lambda_n$ denote the exterior algebra on $n$-generators with its standard grading, and let $Q_n$ denote the associated $k$-linear category.
elements.sent-2411-142: In this case $Q_n$ has object set $\ZZ$, because $\Lambda_n$ is local, and so coincides with the companion category $\sfC_n$.
elements.sent-2411-143: The algebra $\Gamma_n = \Hom_{\sGr \Lambda_n (T,T)$ is, as in \cite{Yamaura *{Example~3.16 , given by the (algebra corresponding to the) full subcategory of $Q_n$ on $0,\ldots,n-1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: For any $k$-algebra $A$ we get equivalences \begin{equation}\sfD_Q(A) \cong \sfD(\Gamma_n \otimes A) \cong \sfD(\Gamma_n' \otimes A)\end{equation} where $\Gamma_n'$ is the Beilinson algebra on $n$ vertices, which is given by the quiver \begin{equation}\xymatrix{ 0 \ar@/^1pc/[r]^-{x_0} \ar@/_1pc/[r]_-{x_{n-1}} \ar@{}[r]|{\vdots} 1 \ar@/^1pc/[r]^-{x_0} \ar@/_1pc/[r]_-{x_{n-1}} \ar@{}[r]|{\vdots} 2 \ar@{}[r]|{ \cdots } n-2 \ar@/^1pc/[r]^-{x_{0}} \ar@/_1pc/[r]_-{x_{n-1}} \ar@{}[r]|{\vdots} n-1 }\end{equation} with commutativity relations $x_ix_j = x_jx_i$ at each pair of consecutive vertices.
elements.sent-2411-145: This is just the Koszul dual of the Koszul algebra $\Gamma_n$ given by swapping anticommutativity relations for commutativity ones.
elements.sent-2411-146: This explains the derived equivalence between $\Gamma_n \otimes A$ and $\Gamma_n' \otimes A$.
elements.sent-2411-147: In particular, if $A$ is commutative we obtain an equivalence $\sfD_Q(A) \cong \sfD(\PP^{n-1 _A)$ via the standard tilting object for $\PP^{n-1 _A$ coming from the twisting sheaves (Beilinson's original derived equivalence \cite{Beilinson over a field generalizes directly to an arbitrary commutative base ring).}}}
elements.sent-2411-148: Jasso recently provided an alternative proof of the theorem, see \cite[cor.\ 3.28 and exa.\ 3.32]{Jasso .}
